{{Short description|Vector field that describes magnetic influence on moving charges}}
{{Infobox physical quantity
| name = Magnetic field
| image = VFPt cylindrical magnet.svg
| caption = Magnetic field lines of a bar magnet, visualized by iron filings. Field lines go from north (N) to south (S) outside the magnet.
| symbols = <math>\mathbf{B}</math>
| SI_unit = [[tesla (unit)|tesla]] (T = N·A⁻¹·m⁻¹ = Wb/m²)
}}

A '''magnetic field''' (sometimes called the '''B-field''') is a [[vector field]] that describes the magnetic influence on moving [[electric charge]]s, [[electric current]]s, and magnetic materials. A moving charge in a magnetic field experiences a force perpendicular to its own velocity and to the magnetic field.

Magnetic fields are produced by moving electric charges ([[electric current]]s) and by the intrinsic [[magnetic moment]]s of [[elementary particle]]s associated with their [[spin (physics)|spin]]. In [[Special relativity|special relativity]], electric and magnetic fields are two interrelated aspects of a single object, the [[electromagnetic tensor]].

== The B-field ==
The magnetic field <math>\mathbf{B}</math> is defined by the [[Lorentz force]] law:
:<math>\mathbf{F} = q(\mathbf{E} + \mathbf{v} \times \mathbf{B})</math>

The SI unit of magnetic field is the [[tesla (unit)|tesla]] (T). Another common unit is the [[Gauss (unit)|gauss]] (G): 1 T = 10,000 G. Earth's surface magnetic field is about 0.5 G (50 μT).

== Sources of magnetic fields ==

=== Moving charges and currents ===
The magnetic field due to a moving point charge ([[Biot–Savart law]]):
:<math>\mathbf{B}(\mathbf{r}) = \frac{\mu_0}{4\pi}\frac{q\mathbf{v} \times \hat{\mathbf{r}}}{r^2}</math>

For a current element <math>I d\mathbf{l}</math>:
:<math>d\mathbf{B} = \frac{\mu_0}{4\pi}\frac{I d\mathbf{l} \times \hat{\mathbf{r}}}{r^2}</math>

=== Ampère's law ===
{{Main|Ampère's circuital law}}
For steady currents:
:<math>\oint \mathbf{B} \cdot d\mathbf{l} = \mu_0 I_{\text{enc}}</math>

In differential form (with Maxwell's correction):
:<math>\nabla \times \mathbf{B} = \mu_0\mathbf{J} + \mu_0\varepsilon_0\frac{\partial\mathbf{E}}{\partial t}</math>

== Magnetic field of common configurations ==

{| class="wikitable"
|-
! Configuration !! Field
|-
| Straight wire || <math>B = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r}</math> (distance <math>r</math>)
|-
| Circular loop (center) || <math>B = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2R}</math>
|-
| Solenoid (inside) || <math>B = \mu_0 n I</math> (<math>n</math> turns per unit length)
|-
| Toroid || <math>B = \frac{\mu_0 NI}{2\pi r}</math>
|}

== Gauss's law for magnetism ==
{{Main|Gauss's law for magnetism}}
Unlike electric fields, magnetic fields have no sources or sinks (no magnetic monopoles):
:<math>\nabla \cdot \mathbf{B} = 0</math>
:<math>\oiint \mathbf{B} \cdot d\mathbf{A} = 0</math>

Magnetic field lines always form closed loops.

== H-field ==
{{Main|Magnetic field#The H-field}}
In materials, the [[magnetization]] <math>\mathbf{M}</math> contributes to the total field. The auxiliary '''H-field''' is:
:<math>\mathbf{H} = \frac{\mathbf{B}}{\mu_0} - \mathbf{M}</math>

For linear magnetic materials, <math>\mathbf{B} = \mu \mathbf{H}</math> where <math>\mu = \mu_r \mu_0</math> is the [[permeability (electromagnetism)|permeability]].

== Force on a current-carrying wire ==
A straight wire of length <math>L</math> carrying current <math>I</math> in a magnetic field <math>\mathbf{B}</math> experiences:
:<math>\mathbf{F} = I\mathbf{L} \times \mathbf{B}</math>

== Energy in magnetic fields ==
The energy density stored in a magnetic field is:
:<math>u_m = \frac{1}{2\mu_0}B^2</math>

== See also ==
{{Portal|Physics}}
* [[Electric field]]
* [[Lorentz force]]
* [[Electromagnetic induction]]
* [[Magnetism]]

== References ==
{{Reflist|30em}}

[[Category:Magnetic field| ]]
[[Category:Magnetism]]
[[Category:Electromagnetism]]
